pattern_summary: A closed transaction loop has been identified involving 4–6 accounts cycling funds in a tight 2–4 hour window. Each hop reduces the amount by approximately 8%, consistent with a layering fee structure used to obscure the criminal origin of funds before integration into the financial system.
evidence: 4–6 accounts form a closed transaction loop with $10K–$50K cycled in under 4 hours
Each hop reduces the amount by ~8%, consistent with automated layering fees in laundering schemes
All timestamps cluster within a tight 2–4 hour window, indicating scripted or bot-driven execution
Originating deposit sourced from an unverified high-risk jurisdiction
No legitimate business purpose identified for the circular fund flow
risk_indicators: Velocity anomaly: 4–6 high-value transfers in under 4 hours across connected accounts
Circular topology: funds return to origin account after multi-hop layering
8% fee deduction per hop matches known professional laundering service rates
regulatory_flags: SAR filing required under BSA — circular layering scheme exceeds $5,000 threshold
Potential violation of 18 U.S.C. § 1956 (Money Laundering) — concealment of proceeds
recommendations: Immediately freeze all accounts in the ring pending Suspicious Activity Report (SAR) filing
Trace the originating deposit to identify the criminal source and upstream beneficiary
Flag all counterparties for enhanced due diligence and 90-day transaction monitoring
Coordinate with FinCEN and law enforcement if total amount exceeds $25,000

pattern_summary: The source account dispersed funds to 5+ separate accounts in transactions deliberately kept just below the $10,000 CTR reporting threshold. All recipient accounts then consolidated funds to a single collection account, forming a classic smurfing pattern used to avoid BSA reporting obligations.
evidence: Source account sent $9,000–$9,800 to 5 separate accounts within 60 minutes — just below the $10,000 CTR threshold
All 5 recipient accounts forwarded funds to a single collection account within 6 hours
Recipient accounts are newly created (< 7 days old), a strong mule account indicator
Transaction amounts show deliberate rounding to stay under reporting thresholds
No prior relationship between source and recipient accounts detected
risk_indicators: Sub-threshold structuring: all transactions deliberately kept under $10,000 CTR limit
Fan-out pattern: 1 source to 5+ recipients followed by immediate consolidation
Newly opened recipient accounts with no prior transaction history
regulatory_flags: Structuring is a federal offense under 31 U.S.C. § 5324 regardless of source of funds
Currency Transaction Report (CTR) may be required for aggregate daily amounts exceeding $10,000
recommendations: File a Suspicious Activity Report (SAR) with FinCEN for confirmed structuring activity
Block the collection (sink) account and place funds on legal hold pending investigation
Escalate source account for full KYC review and beneficial ownership verification
Apply 180-day enhanced monitoring to all accounts in the fan-out network

pattern_summary: A central mule account received a large deposit and dispersed funds to 10+ newly created accounts within 30 minutes. This burst pattern is characteristic of an organized money mule network using synthetic identities to rapidly move and obscure illicit funds before detection systems trigger.
evidence: Central mule account received a $20K–$80K deposit then dispersed funds to 10+ accounts within 30 minutes
All receiving accounts were created within the past 7 days — classic synthetic identity fraud profile
Transaction velocity (10+ outbound transfers in 30 minutes) exceeds normal behavior by 20x
Receiving accounts show no prior activity or established banking history
Geographic dispersion of recipient accounts inconsistent with legitimate payroll or business disbursement
risk_indicators: Burst disbursement: 10+ outbound transfers within 30 minutes from a single account
Synthetic identity indicators on all downstream accounts (new, no history, high velocity)
Hub-and-spoke network topology consistent with professional mule network operations
regulatory_flags: FDIC notification required — potential organized fraud ring activity exceeding $10,000
BSA SAR filing required within 30 days of detection — mass account fraud scheme
recommendations: Block the central mule and all 10+ downstream accounts immediately to prevent further movement
Initiate ACH clawback of funds within the 5-business-day reversal window
Report to FDIC and coordinate with originating bank's fraud team for network takedown
Preserve all transaction logs and account records for law enforcement handoff
